content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

June 27, 1980


Edythe Campbell
City Clerk
2180 Milvia Street
Berkeley, CA 94704

Dear Ms. Campbell:

This is in response to your inquiry of April 24, 1980, regarding your responsibilities in connection with statements of economic interests filed with your office. Specifically, you ask what are your duties when you believe that a filed statement is incomplete and you are not the filing officer.

Commission regulation 18115 delineates the duties and responsibilities of filing officers and those officials who receive statements for the purpose of filing with the filing officer. With respect to filing officers, the regulation provides that the filing officer shall,

(4) Report apparent violations of the Political Reform Act to the appropriate agencies. An apparent violation exists when:

(A) The filing officer knows or has reason to believe that the statement contains material inaccuracies or omissions;

(B) A filer fails to file all or part of his statement or refuses to file all or part of his statement after reasonable notice has been provided by the filing officer.

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18115(a)(4)(A) & (B)

The requirements set forth above pertain only to those officials who are filing officers. The regulation does not impose a similar requirement on officials who only receive

Ms. Edythe Campbell
June 27, 1980
Page Two


statements of economic interests for filing with the filing officer.
Therefore, you are under no duty to report omissions or errors in
those statements for which you are not the filing officer.

Although the Act does not impose upon you a duty to report
apparent violations, it is not in the public interest to allow
violations of this Act or any other law to go unreported by a
public official who has knowledge of the violation. Such action
establishes a bad precedent and further erodes the public's con-
fidence in their government. Therefore, our office strongly
encourages you to report any violations of the Act of which you
are aware even if you are under no legal obligation to do so.

I hope that this advice has been helpful. If I can be of
further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Dwight E. Dickerson
Legal Counsel
Legal Division

DED:nrw

City of Berkeley
CITY CLERK DEPARTMENT
2180 MILVIA STREET
BERKELEY, CALIFORNIA 94704

(415) 641-6360

April 24, 1980


Mr. Ted Pria
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Attention Legal Division

Dear Ted:

I would appreciate advice from the FPPC regarding the extent of my
responsibilities in connection with the Economic Interest Statements
filed with me by Berkeley elected officials.

Specifically, I would appreciate being advised what my position
should be when I have reason to believe that a filed statement is
incomplete or reportable items have been omitted.

FPPC Regulation 18115, adopted September 6, 1978, makes a distinction
between filing officers and an official receiving statements. In the
instance cited, I am the official receiving the statement and forward-
ing it to FPPC. While the filing officer is obligated to report
apparent violations, this obligation is not repeated in the section
covering duties of officials receiving statements.

I have consulted with the Berkeley City Attorney on this matter, and
it is at his suggestion that I seek the advice of your office.

Sincerely,

EDYTHE CAMPBELL
City Clerk

EC mjs

cc: Acting City Attorney

sections.question: What are the responsibilities of a City Clerk as an official receiving Economic Interest Statements filed by elected officials, particularly when the Clerk has reason to believe that a filed statement is incomplete or reportable items have been omitted? Specifically, does the obligation to report apparent violations apply to officials receiving statements, or only to filing officers?
sections.conclusion: The filing officer has an obligation to report apparent violations under Political Reform Act regulations. However, the Act does not impose an apparent violations reporting obligation on officials who are only receiving and forwarding statements. An official receiving statements should not establish a 'bad faith' position by failing to report information they are aware of, and the FPPC encourages proper handling of statements; however, the Act does not explicitly require such officials to report violations.
sections.question_synthetic: The City Clerk of Berkeley seeks clarification on her responsibilities regarding Economic Interest Statements filed by elected officials, specifically whether she is obligated to report apparent violations or incomplete statements to the FPPC, given that FPPC Regulation 18115 distinguishes between filing officers and officials receiving statements.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: While filing officers must report apparent violations, the Political Reform Act does not explicitly require officials merely receiving and forwarding Economic Interest Statements to report violations. However, officials should act in good faith and in accordance with the Act's intent when they become aware of potential violations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document quality is poor with significant OCR degradation making some sections illegible. The incoming request from the City Clerk is clearer than the FPPC response. Extracted question from the incoming request letter and conclusion from the FPPC response letter (page two). Confidence is moderate due to document degradation and fragmented text in the response section.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the responsibilities of a City Clerk as an official receiving Economic Interest Statements filed by elected officials, particularly when the Clerk has reason to believe that a filed statement is incomplete or reportable items have been omitted? Specifically, does the obligation to report apparent violations apply to officials receiving statements, or only to filing officers?

CONCLUSION: The filing officer has an obligation to report apparent violations under Political Reform Act regulations. However, the Act does not impose an apparent violations reporting obligation on officials who are only receiving and forwarding statements. An official receiving statements should not establish a 'bad faith' position by failing to report information they are aware of, and the FPPC encourages proper handling of statements; however, the Act does not explicitly require such officials to report violations.

embedding.summary: The City Clerk of Berkeley inquired about her responsibilities regarding Economic Interest Statements filed by elected officials, specifically whether she must report incomplete statements or apparent violations. The FPPC responded that while filing officers are obligated to report violations, officials receiving statements do not have an explicit obligation to do so, though they should act in good faith.
